title: Superior National Forest Restoration Act of 2025
short_title: Superior National Forest Restoration Act of 2025
plain_summary: This bill cancels a government order that stopped mining in parts of Minnesota's Superior National Forest. It requires the government to quickly review and restore mining leases and permits that were previously canceled. The bill also sets strict deadlines for the government to review new mining applications and gives mining companies the right to automatically renew their leases. The goal is to make it easier and faster for companies to mine in this area of Minnesota.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Natural Resources.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Superior National Forest Restoration Act of two thousand twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2  Superior National Forest System Lands in Minnesota
sections.text: This section cancels an order that stopped mining activities in parts of Minnesota and sets new rules for mining in the Superior National Forest. First, the government cancels Public Land Order Number seven thousand nine hundred seventeen. This order was issued by the Bureau of Land Management on January thirty first, two thousand twenty three. It withdrew federal lands in Cook, Lake, and Saint Louis Counties in Minnesota from mining activities. Second, the Secretary must review all Mine Plans of Operations quickly. A Mine Plan of Operations is a detailed plan that shows how a company will mine an area safely and responsibly. For any mining plan submitted in the ten years after this law passes, the Secretary must finish all environmental and regulatory reviews within eighteen months. This includes reviews required by the National Environmental Policy Act of nineteen sixty nine, which makes sure the government studies how projects might affect the environment. If a company needs to submit additional reviews for their mining plan, the Secretary must review those within six months. The Secretary must also issue all necessary permits within six months after approving the mining plan. Third, the Secretary must bring back all mineral leases, preference right leases, and prospecting permits that were canceled between January thirty first, two thousand twenty one and when this law passes. A mineral lease gives a company the right to mine on government land. A preference right lease gives someone who found valuable minerals the first chance to get a mining lease. A prospecting permit allows someone to explore for minerals. These leases and permits must be brought back with the same terms they had when they were canceled, but with some changes. Prospecting permits get their original terms from when they were first issued. All mineral leases and preference right leases get a new twenty year term starting when this law passes. They also get the automatic right to renew five more times for ten years each. Companies can also negotiate reasonable changes to rental and royalty payments when they renew. Courts cannot review these reissued leases and permits. Fourth, within five days of this law passing, the Secretary must grant preference right leases to anyone whose application was rejected between January thirty first, two thousand twenty one and when this law passes, if they received a notice that valuable minerals were found. These leases get the same terms described above. Fifth, the Secretary can issue permits for using surface lands that are not included in these leases if the surface use is connected to and reasonably necessary for exploring, developing, and using the mineral deposits. The Secretary of the Interior and Secretary of Agriculture must work together on this. Finally, in this section, Secretary means the Secretary of the Interior, or when talking about National Forest System lands, it means the Secretary of Agriculture.
